data.title: Biggest stock movers Thursday: TSM, LLY, AA, and more
data.description:
Stock futures mixed amid renewed optimism over potential negotiated settlements between the U.S. and key trading partners on tariff-related disputes.

Here are some of Thursday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Taiwan Semiconductor (NYSE:TSM) +5% – Shares advanced after the chipmaker posted mixed Q1 results, with profit surging 60% Y/Y, surpassing expectations on strong demand for AI chips. The company issued upbeat guidance, forecasting Q2 revenue between $28.4B and $29.2B, above the $27.22B consensus. For 2025, TSMC expects revenue growth in the mid-20s% range in U.S. dollar terms. Looking further ahead, management projects a revenue CAGR of nearly 20% from 2024 to 2029, a long-term gross margin of 53% or higher, and a return on equity above 25% through the cycle.
  • MongoDB (NASDAQ:MDB) +2.5% – Shares edged higher after Redburn upgraded the database software provider to Neutral from Sell, citing limited downside risk. Analyst Alexander Haissl noted that MongoDB’s valuation multiple is near all-time lows and earnings expectations have already been reset. While macro-related risks persist, Haissl said they are not specific to the company. The firm set a price target of $170, implying a 6.2% upside from the last close.
  • Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) +16% – Shares soared after its once-daily oral GLP-1 drug, orforglipron, met primary and key secondary endpoints in a Phase 3 trial for type 2 diabetes, showing an average weight loss of ~8% (16 lbs) at the highest dose over 40 weeks. The positive data boosted investor confidence in Lilly’s ability to compete in the obesity drug market, pressuring rival Novo Nordisk (NVO) -8%, while peers Viking (VKTX) and Amgen (AMGN) also declined nearly 4%. Lilly expects to submit regulatory filings by 2025–26, pending further data from ongoing trials.
  • Hertz Global (NASDAQ:HTZ) +28% – Shares surged after Pershing Square disclosed a 4.1% stake in Q1, making it the second-largest shareholder behind Knighthead Capital (58.9%). The move sparked speculation of an activist push, with BofA noting that Bill Ackman’s firm could press for operational changes and board representation. Analyst John Babcock added that the rally may give Hertz flexibility to tap equity markets, as it seeks over $500M in capital. Meanwhile, Morgan Stanley's Adam Jonas highlighted Hertz’s strategic value in an AI-driven future, citing its infrastructure potential in supporting autonomous vehicle networks.

Biggest stock losers

  • Alcoa (NYSE:AA) -3% – Shares slipped after the company posted mixed Q1 results and warned that tariff-related costs are set to rise sharply. Alcoa expects to incur $90M in costs this quarter from U.S. tariffs on aluminum imports from Canada, up from $20M in Q1. CEO William Oplinger noted that about 70% of the company’s Canadian aluminum is shipped to the U.S. and now faces a 25% tariff, weighing on profits by an estimated $100M annually. He added that steep U.S. tariffs on Chinese inputs could further increase annual costs by $10M–$15M due to limited alternative suppliers. While higher domestic aluminum prices may provide some relief, Oplinger said ramping up U.S. production remains impractical in the short term, undercutting a key goal of the Trump administration's tariff policy.
  • UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH) -13% – Shares tumbled after the health insurer slashed its full-year adjusted EPS guidance to $26.00–$26.50, down from $29.50–$30.00 and below the $29.74 consensus, following weaker-than-expected Q1 results. The disappointing update dragged down the broader managed care space, with Cigna (CI) down 3% and CVS Health (CVS) and Humana (HUM) -6%.
  • Global Payments (NYSE:GPN) -15% – Shares plunged after the company announced it will acquire the remaining 55% stake in Worldpay from private equity firm GTCR and the remaining 45% from FIS (FIS) for a total transaction value of $24.25B, including $1.55B in anticipated tax assets. At the same time, GPN is offloading its Issuer Solutions business to FIS for $13.5B, with FIS funding the acquisition through a mix of $8B in new debt and proceeds from the Worldpay sale. The complex deal structure raised investor concerns over execution risk, integration complexity, and elevated leverage, contributing to the sharp selloff in GPN shares. Meanwhile, FIS rose 6%, as the transaction allows it to fully monetize a non-cash-generating asset while gaining a high-margin, recurring revenue business expected to enhance adjusted EBITDA margins, EPS, and cash flow within 12 months, with $125M+ in long-term revenue synergies and $150M+ in EBITDA synergies projected by year three.

data.title: D.R. Horton revises down year guidance as homebuilder's FQ2 earnings fall short
data.description:
D.R. Horton (NYSE:DHI) stock gained 2% in Thursday morning trading, reversing the 3% dip during the premarket session, even after delivering below-consensus fiscal-year 2025 guidance and posting worse-than-expected earnings and revenue for the FQ2.

The U.S.'s largest homebuilder now expects fiscal 2025 revenue of $33.3B-$34.8B (midpoint $34.1B vs. $36.1B consensus). That's down from the prior outlook of $36B-$37.5B. Homes closed by homebuilding operations are anticipated to be 85K-87K (midpoint 86K vs. 91K in prior guide), and cash flow provided by operations are targeted at more than $3.0B.

It expects to buy back about $4.0B of its shares during the year.

GAAP EPS for the quarter ended March 31, 2025, fell to $2.58 from $2.61 in Q1 and $3.52 a year before, missing the $2.65 average analyst estimate.

“The 2025 spring selling season started slower than expected as potential homebuyers have been more cautious due to continued affordability constraints and declining consumer confidence," Executive Chairman David Auld said in a statement.

Q2 homebuilding net sales orders of 22,437 with a value of $8.36B rose from 17,837 ($6.7B in Q1) but retreated from 26,456 ($10.1B) in Q1 2024. Cancellation rate was 16% vs. 18% in the previous quarter and 15% a year before.

At March 31, the company had 36,900 homes in inventory, of which 23,500 were unsold. Some 8,400 of the D.R. Horton's (NYSE:DHI) unsold homes at the end of the three-month period were completed, of which 1,200 had been completed in greater than six months.

Its land and lot portfolio totaled 613,100 lots at the end of Q2, of which 25% were owned and 75% were controlled through land and lot purchase contracts.

During the quarter, DHI repurchased 9.7M common shares for $1.3B and paid cash dividend of $125.5M. In April, the board authorized a new share repurchase program of $5.0B, replacing the previous authorization, which at that time had $1.1B remaining due to buybacks made after quarter end.

data.title: Tetra Tech awarded contract for Oklahoma’s first indirect potable reuse facility
data.description:
• Tetra Tech (NASDAQ:TTEK) on Thursday said the City of Bartlesville, Oklahoma, has awarded the company a contract to provide engineering design and technical services for the first indirect potable reuse project to be permitted within the State of Oklahoma.
  • Building upon Tetra Tech’s initial IPR pilot study, Tetra Tech engineers and technical specialists will now design innovative IPR solutions to expand the Chickasaw Wastewater Treatment Plant’s capacity by 15%. 
  • TTEK is +0.55% to $30.015.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Spirit Airlines names airline executive as new CEO
data.description:
Spirit Airlines (OTC:SAVEQ) has appointed Sun Country (SNCY) CFO Dave Davis as the budget carrier’s new chief executive, handing the seasoned airline executive the daunting task of ushering the carrier back to profitability during a time of economic uncertainty and stiff competition for the discount traveler from larger rivals.

Davis replaces Ted Christie, who left the company on April 7 after emerging from Chapter 11 bankruptcy.

According to the Wall Street Journal, Spirit (OTC:SAVEQ) is also bringing aboard other airline executives to help navigate the business including former JetBlue (JBLU) and TAP Air Portugal executive Trey Urbahn to help with commercial strategy, and Duncan Dee, former COO at Air Canada to oversee communications.

As part of the company’s bankruptcy restructuring, ~$795M of funded debt was converted into equity. The company also received a $350M equity investment from existing investors to support future initiatives including enhanced travel experiences and greater value. These initiatives include new premium offerings and enhanced benefits to lure in more affluent travelers, including checked bags, no change fees, and seat preference as part of its “Go Big, Go Comfy, Go Savvy” offerings.

The company’s shares are currently listed on the over-the-counter exchange, with plans to re-list on the NYSE as soon as reasonably practicable.

data.title: Nurix gets FDA clearance to initiate trial for its anti-inflammatory drug candidate
data.description:
• Nurix Therapeutics (NASDAQ:NRIX) said on Thursday that the U.S. FDA has cleared the IND for GS-6791/NX-0479, an experimental drug designed to break down IRAK4 protein for inflammatory conditions.
  • The acceptance enables the initiation of a Phase 1 trial, which is anticipated to begin in Q2 2025, the company said.
  • The drug is a part of a collaboration between Nurix and Gilead Sciences (NASDAQ:GILD), who licensed the program in 2023 and is responsible for advancing this program through clinical development.
  • As part of this agreement, Nurix will get a $5M milestone payment from Gilead for FDA clearance of the IND, bringing the total amount received under the 2019 collaboration agreement to $135M.

data.title: Short bets against S&P 500 healthcare stock rises in March; MRNA stays top shorted
data.description:
In March, short sellers collectively increased their negative bets on the broader healthcare industry, as indicated by a rise in the average short interest for the S&P 500 Health Care Index Sector (NYSEARCA:XLV) to 1.81%, up from 1.79% in February. Within the sector, Moderna maintained its position as the stock with the highest level of short interest for the month.

The healthcare sector, as represented by the Health Care Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLV) which holds a 12.12% weight in the S&P 500, has experienced a relatively smaller decline of 0.93% since the start of the year. This contrasts with the broader S&P 500 index (SP500), which has seen a more significant decrease of 10.30% during the same period.

 

Stocks with the largest and least short positions

Ranked by short interest as a percentage of shares float

Moderna (NASDAQ:MRNA)—most shorted—saw short interest rose to 14.58% from 13.19% in February. Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Ratings, the biotechnology company focused on developing messenger RNA ((mRNA)) therapeutics and vaccines has a Strong Sell rating with a score of 1.19 out of 5.

Seeking Alpha analysts Stephen Ayers and Terry Chrisomalis discussed the potential impact of management changes at a regulatory agency on vaccine makers. Ayers believes the Trump administration's stance on vaccines could create market uncertainty, as seen in Moderna's (NASDAQ:MRNA) stock drop. Ayers also notes a potential "snowball effect" for mRNA companies due to existing public wariness, which could be worsened by increased regulatory scrutiny, impacting the entire vaccine lifecycle from development to market adoption. Increased regulatory scrutiny could affect vaccine developers like Moderna, Pfizer, and BioNTech, leading to stricter data requirements, extended development timelines, and increased application rejection.

ResMed (NYSE:RMD) came in second with a short interest of 6.10%, followed by DaVita (NYSE:DVA) with a short interest of 5.82%.

Johnson & Johnson (NYSE:JNJ) was the least shorted stock in March, with a short interest of 0.79%, followed by Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) which came in a close second with a short percentage of 0.83%. Medtronic (NYSE:MDT) was the third least shorted company in March, with a short percentage of 0.85%.

 

Industry Analysis

Average short interest as a percentage of floating shares

 

For the twelfth consecutive month in March, biotechnology held the position of the most shorted industry within the healthcare index. Short interest in this sub-sector saw a slight increase to 2.63%, up from 2.62% in February. The healthcare provider and services sector followed with a short interest of 1.88% at the end of March. In contrast, the pharmaceuticals industry recorded the lowest short interest among the healthcare sub-sectors in March, with a short percentage of 1.60%.

Related ETFs: Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund (NYSEARCA:XLV), SPDR S&P Pharmaceuticals ETF (NYSEARCA:XPH), VanEck Pharmaceutical ETF (NASDAQ:PPH), iShares Nasdaq Biotechnology ETF (NASDAQ:IBB), iShares U.S. Pharmaceuticals ETF (NYSEARCA:IHE), and Invesco Nasdaq Biotechnology ETF (NASDAQ:IBBQ).

data.title: Better Choice Company reinstates stock buyback program, increases authorization to 6.5M
data.description:
• Better Choice (NYSE:BTTR) has reinstated its stock repurchase program and increased authorization up to $6.5M of its outstanding shares of common stock, until December 31, 2025.
  • The company repurchased 102,405 common shares at an average price of $1.9869 per share under the previous $5M stock repurchase program, which expired on December 31, 2024.
  • BTTR -1.6% premarket to $1.84.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: BP Chair Lund re-elected with significant protest vote
data.description:
BP (NYSE:BP) CEO Murray Auchincloss and Chair Helge Lund were re-elected at Thursday's annual general meeting, Reuters reported, although the lower level of shareholder support for Lund could mean his previously announced departure occurs sooner than the expected 2026 timetable.

BP's (NYSE:BP) board was up for re-election as the company faces activist shareholder Elliott Management and criticism from climate-focused investors that wanted a protest vote against Lund.

Auchincloss received 97% of votes and Lund an unusually low 75.7%, according to provisional results; board members need 50% of votes to be elected and typically achieve tallies near 100%.

Separately, BP (BP) said it has loaded the first cargo of liquefied natural gas for export from its Greater Tortue Ahmeyim Phase 1 project offshore Mauritania and Senegal.

The project has been hampered by technical, financial, and Covid-related issues, and originally was expected to begin LNG exports in 2022.

GTA Phase 1 first LNG is BP's (BP) third major project start-up in 2025, in line with its strategy of growing its upstream oil and gas business.
